validated-composition-organizes-complete.text: The complete, validated module design framework intersects with composition-over-inheritance as a cross-cutting theme validated at multiple abstraction levels — theoretical (LSP undecidability), pattern-level (Abstract Factory principle density), and empirical (modern language adoption). Composition serves as a strong organizing theme across the framework, while the framework's primary validation rests on independent confirmation by both GRASP and SOLID methodologies for its internal quality and external communication subsystems.

justifications.label: Module framework organized by the principle it is validated through at every level

visitor-architecture-determined-by-dispa.text: Visitor's three-participant structure (Visitor, Element, ObjectStructure/Client) aligns closely with the double-dispatch callback mechanism: element.accept(visitor) dispatches on element type, then visitor.visit(element) dispatches on visitor type. This two-step callback sequence naturally involves distinct participant roles — an element to initiate the first dispatch, a visitor to receive the callback for the second dispatch, and a client to compose the structure and trigger traversal — suggesting the triangular relationship is shaped by the dispatch mechanism rather than being an arbitrary design choice.

justifications.label: The three participants are not independent design decisions but structural consequences of simulating double dispatch through two sequential single dispatches

visitor-dissolves-in-richer-type-systems.text: The Visitor pattern dissolves entirely in languages with either multiple dispatch or sum types with pattern matching, revealing it as a workaround for single-dispatch type system limitations rather than a fundamental design concept.

justifications.label: two independent language features each independently eliminate the need for Visitor

visitor-pattern-implements-double-dispat.text: The Visitor pattern is the standard design pattern used to implement double dispatch in single-dispatch languages like C++ and Java via the accept/visit two-step callback mechanism

visitor-pattern-implements-open-closed-p.text: The Visitor pattern is a primary mechanism for satisfying the Open/Closed Principle: element classes are closed for modification but open for extension via new visitor implementations.

visitor-pattern-is-gof-behavioral.text: The Visitor pattern is one of the twenty-three Gang of Four design patterns, classified as a behavioral pattern.

visitor-pattern-multiple-dispatch-dissol.text: In languages supporting multiple dispatch (e.g., Common Lisp), the Visitor pattern dissolves into ordinary generic function overloading with no accept/visit ceremony needed.

visitor-pattern-separates-algorithm-from.text: The Visitor pattern separates an algorithm from the object structure it operates on, allowing new operations to be added without modifying element classes.

visitor-pattern-three-participants.text: The Visitor pattern has three participants: Visitor (declares visit methods per element type), Element (declares accept(visitor) calling visitor.visit(this)), and Client (constructs object structure and initiates traversal).

visitor-pattern-trade-off-new-ops-vs-new.text: The Visitor pattern makes adding new operations easy (add a new visitor) but adding new element types hard (every existing visitor must be updated).

visitor-pattern-unnecessary-with-sum-typ.text: Languages with sum types and pattern matching (e.g., ML, Rust, Haskell) largely obviate the Visitor pattern because the compiler enforces exhaustive case handling natively.

visitor-pattern-uses-double-dispatch.text: The Visitor pattern simulates double dispatch in single-dispatch languages: element.accept(visitor) dispatches on element type, then visitor.visit(this) dispatches on visitor type.

vtable-mechanism-for-dynamic-dispatch.text: In most OO languages, subtype polymorphism is implemented via a vtable (virtual table): each class has a table of function pointers, and each object holds a pointer to its class's vtable, enabling late binding.

webobjects-1996-drove-web-mvc-adoption.text: NeXT's WebObjects (1996), originally written in Objective-C, drove MVC adoption in web development.

wrapper-patterns-complete-without-forwar.text: The four structural wrapper patterns (Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy) — unified by delegation and LoD enforcement — provide architecturally complete coverage of all object-wrapping needs only when the practical cost of forwarding boilerplate does not deter their adoption, since each additional wrapper layer multiplies the explicit delegation surface.

justifications.label: Wrapper patterns have conceptual completeness but practical completeness is gated by the forwarding cost that scales with delegation depth and interface width

wrapper-taxonomy-governed-by-two-orthogo.text: The four structural wrapper patterns are governed by two orthogonal binary dimensions: interface preservation (Proxy and Decorator preserve the subject's interface vs. Adapter and Facade transform it) and primary intent (Decorator and Adapter modify capability vs. Proxy and Facade modify access topology), yielding a 2×2 design space rather than a flat four-element list.

justifications.label: Four-way wrapper taxonomy reduces to two binary design decisions

wrapper-taxonomy-spans-full-interface-me.text: The four structural wrapper patterns' 2×2 design space (interface preservation vs. transformation × capability vs. access topology) combined with Adapter-Bridge's preventive/corrective distinction provides a framework for classifying interface mediation strategies along both structural and temporal dimensions — most wrapper intents can be located within a pattern quadrant and further distinguished by whether mediation is designed upfront or retrofitted.

justifications.label: 2×2 wrapper taxonomy plus temporal dimension yields complete interface mediation space

yo-yo-problem-deep-inheritance.text: The yo-yo problem results from excessively deep inheritance hierarchies where too many thin layers make debugging and comprehension difficult
